---
title: "Baseline experiment: resting state and the Dolan agonist transient"
subtitle: "Platelet whole-cell model — demo experiment 1 of 4"
author: "Steve Haigh"
date: 2026-06-23
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    geometry: "margin=2.5cm"
    fontsize: 11pt
    mainfont: "STIX Two Text"
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: blue
    urlcolor: blue
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    include-in-header: ../pandoc-header.tex
  html:
    toc: true
    embed-resources: true
---

## Purpose

This is the **baseline** against which the three perturbation experiments
(aspirin, MCU knockout, clopidogrel) are read. It does two things:

1. shows that the model holds a **stable resting state**, and
2. reproduces the **agonist-evoked Ca²⁺ transient** of Dolan & Diamond
   (*Biophys. J.* 2014) when a physiological agonist mixture is applied after a
   60 s settling period, and that the activated state is **maintained** out to
   300 s.

Experiments 2–4 take this exact set-up and change a single knob.

## Model set-up

| Setting | Value |
|---|---|
| Cell | single platelet, +extracellular Ca²⁺ (1.2 mM) |
| Agonists | thrombin 1 nM, ADP 10 µM, ATP 10 µM (the Dolan standard) |
| Agonist onset | t = 60 s (a 60 s settle precedes stimulation) |
| Duration | 300 s |
| `RunConfig` | `agonist_delay_s = 60` (all other knobs at default) |

Reproduce via the TUI (set Length = 300, Agonist delay = 60, the default agonist
levels) or directly:

```bash
runFromConfig.py --config spec.json   # {"length_sec":300,"run_config":{"agonist_delay_s":60}}
analysisPlatelet.py exp_base --plot demo_calcium
```

The agonists enter as transient time-courses (a rise and slow decay), not step
inputs — this matches the *shape* of an agonist bolus reaching a platelet,
though the precise time-course is a modelling choice, not a measurement.

## Resting state

Started from the Dolan initial conditions (cytosolic Ca²⁺ = 100 nM, dense
tubular system (DTS) store = 250 µM), the model does **not** sit exactly still:
those initial conditions are not its own equilibrium, so it undergoes a damped
settling transient — an overshoot to ~128 nM by t ≈ 30 s — before converging to
its resting fixed point of **~95 nM cytosolic / ~255 µM store** by ~300 s
(@fig-resting). Resting IP₃ holds at ~50 nM throughout.

![**Resting state with no agonist (300 s).** Left: cytosolic Ca²⁺ relaxes from the imposed initial value (100 nM) through a damped overshoot (~128 nM at ~30 s) to a maintained fixed point of ~95 nM. Centre: the DTS store mirrors this (250 → ~199 → ~255 µM); store-operated entry stays near its low resting level. Right: IP₃ holds at ~50 nM. The model converges to and then holds a resting steady state close to the Dolan resting targets (100 nM / 250 µM); the settling takes a few hundred seconds because the imposed initial conditions are not the model's exact equilibrium. Grey baseline overlay is absent (single run). Provenance is printed in the figure footnote.](../figures/demos/resting_calcium.png){#fig-resting width=100%}

The practical consequence: at the 60 s mark used below (and by Dolan), the
resting state is still completing this settle. This does not materially affect
the agonist response, but it is the honest reason the resting trace in
@fig-agonist is not perfectly flat over 0–60 s.

## Agonist response

Applying the agonist mixture at t = 60 s evokes the canonical response
(@fig-agonist): cytosolic Ca²⁺ rises and is sustained as a **plateau of
~423 nM** (held from ~150 s to 300 s), the DTS store empties (250 → ~1 µM),
store-operated entry switches on, and IP₃ rises to ~220 nM. Downstream, the
αIIbβ3 integrin reaches a PAC-1⁺ active fraction of ~0.80 and thromboxane A₂
rises to ~1 µM (used as the references for experiments 2–4).

![**Resting state then Dolan agonist transient (agonist at 60 s, 300 s total).** Left: cytosolic Ca²⁺ — the brief resting settle (0–60 s) is followed by a rise to a sustained ~423 nM plateau that is maintained to 300 s. Centre: the DTS store empties (~250 → ~1 µM) as IP₃ receptors open, and store-operated Ca²⁺ entry (right axis, dashed) switches on to sustain the cytosolic plateau. Right: endogenously generated IP₃ rises to ~220 nM. The sustained (rather than transient-then-recovering) plateau is the model's autocrine "second wave" under a saturating agonist; it is store-limited, so the peak height is set by buffering and SOCE rather than by the exact agonist dose.](../figures/demos/baseline_calcium.png){#fig-agonist width=100%}

## Comparison with real platelets

- **Resting Ca²⁺.** Real resting platelets sit at ~50–100 nM cytosolic Ca²⁺ with
  a high DTS store; the model's resting fixed point (~95 nM / ~255 µM) is within
  this range and close to the Dolan targets. The damped settling transient is a
  model artefact of the chosen initial conditions, not a biological prediction.
- **Agonist transient.** Real platelets respond to thrombin/ADP with a rapid
  cytosolic Ca²⁺ rise to several hundred nM, sustained in the presence of
  extracellular Ca²⁺ by store-operated entry — qualitatively what the model
  shows, and the basis of its validation against Dolan & Diamond (2014) Fig. 4
  (the "5/5" peak/SOCE-differential criteria).
- **Honest differences.** (i) The model's store empties almost completely
  (~1 µM), whereas Dolan infer a substantial residual; the free luminal store
  Ca²⁺ has never been measured directly, so this is a falsifiable model
  prediction, not a known error. (ii) The real transient is sharper and shows
  more recovery; the model's sustained plateau reflects its autocrine
  amplification loops under a saturating agonist. (iii) Cytosolic free Ca²⁺ is
  **store-limited** in the model — the peak is nearly insensitive to most single
  parameters — which is the key caveat for interpreting experiments 2–4.

## Take-away

The model holds a physiologically reasonable resting state and reproduces the
Dolan agonist transient and a maintained activated plateau. The next three
documents perturb this baseline by one knob each — aspirin (COX-1), MCU
knockout, and clopidogrel (P2Y12) — and read the change against these reference
values.
